findings.why: build_where interpolates keyword, status, and user_id directly into SQL passed to raw_query
limit and offset are interpolated into the SQL string via f-string
create_order fetches attacker-controlled callback_url with no host/scheme validation
verify_tls defaults to false, so outbound HTTP requests skip TLS certificate verification
httpx.Client verify follows settings.verify_tls which defaults to disabled
make_token uses random.choice (non-cryptographic PRNG) for session and reset tokens
admin routes have no require_user or is_admin check, exposing cross-tenant operations to anyone
get_order returns any order by id without checking ownership against the authenticated user
update_order mutates any order by id without verifying it belongs to the caller
update_order applies arbitrary OrderUpdate fields including user_id, status, amount_cents from client input
OrderUpdate exposes user_id, amount_cents, and status for client-controlled updates
confirm_reset does not revoke existing sessions after password change, violating documented invariant
change_password does not revoke existing sessions after credential change, violating documented invariant
reset request rate limit keyed by email allows enumeration/abuse and confirm limiter uses a static key
request.url.path logged unsanitized allows CRLF/log forging
build_where interpolates user_id, keyword, and status directly into SQL string passed to raw_query, and limit/offset are f-string-interpolated too
update_order builds column names into the SET clause from dict keys derived from client-controlled OrderUpdate fields
make_token uses random.choice (non-CSPRNG) for session tokens and password-reset tokens
verify_tls defaults to false, so outbound HTTP requests skip TLS certificate verification by default
create_order fetches user-supplied callback_url with no scheme/IP allowlist, enabling SSRF to internal/metadata endpoints
OrderUpdate exposes amount_cents and user_id, and update_order blindly applies them allowing price/ownership manipulation
get_order and update_order fetch/mutate by order_id with no ownership/tenant check on the authenticated user
admin router endpoints have no require_user or admin authorization dependency, exposing cross-tenant order listing and status changes to anyone
set_status updates any order by id with no authorization check
PasswordHasher() uses default parameters and no check_needs_rehash / rehash-on-login
reset token lookup compares SHA-256 hex via SQL equality but token is low-entropy from non-CSPRNG; no constant-time compare and token generation is insecure
confirm_reset sets a new password but never revokes existing sessions (revoke_all_for_user documented but not called) and does not enforce single-use before commit atomically
change_password does not revoke other sessions after credential change despite revoke_all_for_user being the documented invariant
on_error logs request.url.path (user-controlled) without CR/LF sanitization
database_url defaults to a hardcoded connection string in source
state-changing endpoints rely on bearer header but no anti-CSRF/Origin checks; cookie-based misuse and login-CSRF surface unaddressed across app
TrustedHostMiddleware allowed_hosts=['*'] disables host validation enabling Host-header attacks
request_reset rate-limits per-email allowing enumeration-free but attacker-controlled key bypass and does not rate-limit per-IP; reset does not invalidate on password change elsewhere
